questions.question: How does the WHO define burnout in ICD-11?

options.text: Burnout is a personal character flaw or mental illness

Burnout is an occupational syndrome characterized by three dimensions: exhaustion (emotional/physical depletion), cynicism (detachment/negativity about work), and reduced efficacy (feeling incompetent)
Burnout only affects healthcare workers
Burnout is the same as depression and should be treated with medication

questions.explanation: The WHO recognizes burnout as an occupational syndrome (not a mental illness) in ICD-11, characterized by three dimensions: (1) Exhaustion — emotional and physical depletion, (2) Cynicism — detachment from work, negativity about job, and (3) Reduced efficacy — feeling incompetent or ineffective. Burnout isn't a personal failure — it's a mismatch between job demands and resources. Setting workplace boundaries is a protective factor that prevents chronic work stress from becoming burnout.

questions.question: What is 'scope creep' and why is it a workplace boundary violation?

options.text: Scope creep is when coworkers stand too close to you physically

Scope creep is gradual expansion of your responsibilities beyond your job description without corresponding increase in resources, pay, or time — it violates role boundaries and leads to overextension and burnout
Scope creep is a positive thing that shows you're a valuable employee
Scope creep only happens in tech companies

questions.explanation: Scope creep is the gradual expansion of your responsibilities beyond your job description without corresponding increase in resources, pay, or time. It happens because you're competent, reliable, and struggle to say no — managers assign you more, colleagues ask for help, you become the go-to person. The cost: you're doing 1.5 jobs for the price of one, you can't do any of them well, resentment builds, quality declines, and burnout accelerates. This is a role boundary violation that requires assertive communication and negotiation with your manager.

questions.question: Which communication strategy is most effective for setting boundaries with supervisors?

options.text: Simply say 'I can't do that' and provide no explanation

Use solutions-focused language: 'I want to deliver excellent work on [priority]. Taking on [new request] would compromise quality. Can we talk about priorities or alternatives?' — shows commitment to work while advocating for sustainable workload
Complain that you're overwhelmed without offering any data or solutions
Agree to everything and then quit without notice when you burn out

questions.explanation: The most effective strategy is solutions-focused communication: Don't just say 'no' — offer alternatives or solutions. Template: 'I want to deliver excellent work on [current priority]. Taking on [new request] would compromise that quality. Can we [discuss priorities/adjust timeline/reassign something]?' This shows you're committed to the work (not just protecting yourself) and frames the boundary as service to the organization (quality, sustainable performance). You can also use data-driven advocacy (track hours, projects, response times) to demonstrate you're at capacity with objective evidence.

questions.question: What is compassion fatigue, and how is it different from burnout?

options.text: Compassion fatigue and burnout are exactly the same thing

Compassion fatigue is emotional/physical exhaustion from prolonged exposure to others' suffering (can have sudden onset after traumatic exposure); burnout is gradual from chronic work stress. Both require boundaries but compassion fatigue specifically affects capacity for empathy in caregiving roles
Compassion fatigue only affects people with weak personalities
Burnout is worse than compassion fatigue

questions.explanation: Compassion fatigue is emotional and physical exhaustion from prolonged exposure to others' suffering — common in healthcare, social work, teaching, and caregiving professions. It can have sudden onset after particularly traumatic exposure. Symptoms: emotional numbness, intrusive thoughts about clients/patients, withdrawal, reduced empathy. Burnout is gradual, caused by chronic work stress, affects work engagement generally. You can have both simultaneously. Compassion fatigue specifically affects capacity for empathy. Both require boundaries: emotional (witnessing without over-identifying), role (helper not savior), time (work stays at work), peer support (debriefing), and self-care as ethical imperative.

questions.question: When do workplace boundary issues warrant professional help or escalation beyond self-advocacy?

options.text: Never — you should always handle workplace issues yourself

When experiencing severe burnout affecting functioning (can't get out of bed, suicidal thoughts, substance abuse), workplace harassment/discrimination, unsafe working conditions (OSHA violation), or workplace trauma (witnessed violence, patient death) requiring trauma-informed therapy
Only if you've been formally diagnosed with a mental illness
Anytime you feel stressed at work

questions.explanation: Seek professional help or escalation when: (1) Severe burnout affects your ability to function (can't get out of bed, suicidal thoughts, substance abuse) — use EAP, medical leave (FMLA), or therapy; (2) Workplace harassment or discrimination — contact HR, file EEOC complaint, or consult employment attorney; (3) Unsafe working conditions — report to OSHA; (4) Workplace trauma (witnessed violence, patient death, etc.) — seek trauma-informed therapy (EMDR, CPT). These situations exceed the scope of self-advocacy and require systemic intervention or clinical support. The lesson explicitly provides crisis resources: 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, SAMHSA National Helpline.
